subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Rate & Accumulation (Riemann Sums)
stimulus:
The amount of water in a storage tank, in gallons, is modeled by a continuous function on the time interval $0 \le t \le 7$, where $t$ is measured in hours. In this model, rates are given as follows:

(i) The rate at which water enters the tank is $f(t) = 100t^2 \sin t$ gallons per hour for $0 \le t \le 7$.

(ii) The rate at which water leaves the tank is $g(t) = \begin{cases} 250 & 0 \le t < 3 \\ 2000 & 3 < t \le 7 \end{cases}$ gallons per hour.

The graphs of $f$ and $g$, which intersect at $t = 1.617$ and $t = 5.076$, are shown in the figure above. At time $t = 0$, the amount of water in the tank is 5000 gallons.

parts.prompt: How many gallons of water enter the tank during the time interval $0 \le t \le 7$? Round your answer to the nearest gallon.

scoring_points.description: Correct setup and answer: total water entering
scoring_points.official_rubric: 2 points: 1 point for the integral $\int_0^7 f(t)\,dt$; 1 point for the answer $\approx 8264$ gallons (round to nearest gallon).

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly integrates the inflow rate $f(t) = 100t^2\sin t$ over $[0,7]$ and rounds to the nearest gallon.
Not rounded to the nearest gallon as required; answer must be a whole number.

parts.prompt: For $0 \le t \le 7$, find the time intervals during which the amount of water in the tank is decreasing. Give a reason for each answer.

scoring_points.description: Correct time intervals when water decreasing
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct intervals $0 \le t \le 1.617$ and $3 \le t \le 5.076$ (endpoints may vary slightly).

sample_responses.response: The amount decreases on $0 \le t \le 1.617$ and $3 \le t \le 5.076$.
sample_responses.commentary: Both intervals correctly identified where $f(t) < g(t)$.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: reason that $f(t) < g(t)$ on those intervals (water leaving exceeds water entering).

sample_responses.response: Because $f(t) < g(t)$ for $0 < t < 1.617$ and $3 < t < 5.076$, the rate water leaves exceeds the rate water enters.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly links the decreasing water to $f(t) - g(t) < 0$.

sample_responses.response: Because both rates are positive on those intervals.
sample_responses.commentary: Being positive doesn't explain decrease; must compare $f(t)$ to $g(t)$.

required_elements: The rate leaving exceeds the rate entering on those intervals

scoring_points.description: Complete answer with both intervals and reasons
scoring_points.official_rubric: Combined: earns 2 points total for part (b) — 1 for intervals and 1 for reason.

sample_responses.response: The water is decreasing on $[0, 1.617]$ and $[3, 5.076]$ because $f(t) < g(t)$ on those intervals.
sample_responses.commentary: Both intervals and the reason are provided.

parts.prompt: For $0 \le t \le 7$, at what time $t$ is the amount of water in the tank greatest? To the nearest gallon, compute the amount of water at this time. Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Maximum at t=5.076 where f(t)=g(t) and f-g changes from positive to negative. W(5.076) = 5000 + integral_0^{5.076} [f(t)-g(t)] dt ≈ 4976 gallons. Justification: W'(t) = f(t) - g(t) changes from positive to negative at t=5.076.

scoring_points.description: Identifies $t = 3$ as a candidate and the correct maximum location
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: identifies $t = 3$ as a candidate. Since $f(t) - g(t)$ changes from positive to negative only at $t = 3$, the candidates are $t = 0, 3, 7$. The maximum occurs at $t = 3$.

sample_responses.response: Since $f(t) - g(t)$ changes sign from positive to negative only at $t = 3$, the candidates for absolute maximum are $t = 0, 3, 7$.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies $t = 3$ as the only interior candidate where the net rate changes from positive to negative.

sample_responses.response: The maximum occurs at $t = 5.076$ where $f(t) = g(t)$.
sample_responses.commentary: $t = 5.076$ is where $f = g$ on the second crossing, but water was already decreasing from $t = 3$ to $5.076$ because $g(t) = 2000 > f(t)$ on $(3, 5.076)$.

scoring_points.description: Correct integrand for water amount computation
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct integrand. $W(3) = 5000 + \int_0^3 f(t)\,dt - 250(3) = 5126.591$ gallons; $W(7) = W(3) + \int_3^7 f(t)\,dt - 2000(4) = 4513.807$ gallons.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly integrates net inflow: adds $\int_0^3 f(t)\,dt$ and subtracts outflow $250 \times 3$.

scoring_points.description: Correct numerical answer: greatest at $t = 3$ with $\approx 5127$ gallons
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point (split across amounts at $t=3$ and $t=7$) and 1 point conclusion: greatest at $t = 3$ with $\approx 5127$ gallons.

sample_responses.response: The water is greatest at $t = 3$ hours with approximately $5127$ gallons.
sample_responses.commentary: Compares amounts at all candidate times; $5127 > 5000 > 4514$, so maximum is at $t = 3$.

parts.prompt: Write, but do not evaluate, an integral expression that gives the total amount of water in the tank, in gallons, at time $t = 7$.
parts.scoring_notes: W(7) = 5000 + integral from 0 to 7 of [f(t) - g(t)] dt. Both the initial condition 5000 and the correct net rate integrand are required.

scoring_points.description: Correct net rate integrand $f(t) - g(t)$ in integral from $0$ to $7$
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct integrand $f(t) - g(t)$ integrated from $0$ to $7$.

sample_responses.commentary: Net rate of change of water is $f(t) - g(t)$; integrating from $0$ to $7$ gives total change.
Only accounts for inflow; must subtract outflow $g(t)$ to get net change.

alternatives.correct_example: $\int_0^7 [f(t) - g(t)]\, dt$ with $f(t) = 100t^2\sin t$ and piecewise $g(t)$

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: includes initial condition $5000$ gallons: $W(7) = 5000 + \int_0^7 [f(t) - g(t)]\,dt$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly adds initial 5000 gallons to net change integral.
Omits the initial condition; total gallons requires starting value of 5000.
